decomposition.title: Project Polaris: Evaluation of Hosting Options (Cloud, DCaaS & Hybrid)
decomposition.industry: Manufacturing, enterprise IT, kitchen and bath products, wellness, hospitality, and digital innovation
decomposition.estimated_deal_size_usd: No explicit budget stated. Inferred potential future hosting/transformation opportunity: approximately $15M-$50M+ over a multi-year term based on a large-scale Wisconsin data center exit, enterprise SAP ecosystem, VMware estate, Windows/Linux servers, multiple database platforms, backup/DR, networking, monitoring, security tooling, and global manufacturing integrations. Phase 0 advisory/evaluation-only value would likely be materially smaller, likely below $1M.
decomposition.contract_duration: Not explicitly stated. Inferred: Phase 0 evaluation activity runs from RFP publication on 29 May to supplier presentations in the week of 6 July 2026, approximately 6 weeks; any future hosting/DCaaS/hybrid commercial contract is not specified but would typically be structured over a multi-year term, likely 3-5 years, based on enterprise hosting scope.

requirements.text: Vendors are requested to submit proposals detailing target architecture and platform capabilities.
Vendors are requested to submit proposals detailing high-level transition approach and indicative sequencing.
Vendors are requested to submit proposals detailing operating model and support framework.
Vendors are requested to submit proposals detailing commercial and pricing structure.
Proposed solutions should support enterprise-scale deployments, secure connectivity, and integration with Kohlerâ€™s enterprise and manufacturing ecosystem.

Evaluation should ensure that application performance and availability are maintained or improved, integration with manufacturing and enterprise systems is supported, and transition risks and business disruption are minimized.
Vendors are expected to propose a target operating model including roles and responsibilities and a shared responsibility framework.
Vendors are expected to propose a target operating model including governance, security, and compliance approach.
Vendors are expected to propose a target operating model including operations and support model.
Vendors must clearly articulate the proposed solution covering target-state architecture aligned to multi-account / multi-subscription landing zone principles.
Vendors must clearly articulate the landing zone deployment model and application deployment model.
Vendors must clearly articulate environment segregation for Prod / Test / Dev.
Vendors must clearly articulate identity integration with enterprise identity providers.
Vendors must clearly articulate the hybrid connectivity approach.
Vendors must clearly articulate region strategy and data residency approach.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Data residency is a common hidden disqualifier area. This RFP requires a region strategy and data residency approach, but does not state exact jurisdictions in the main document.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Region strategy and data residency approach required; no specific country, state, or sovereignty restriction stated in the provided document.

requirements.text: Vendors must clearly articulate disaster recovery, high availability, and scalability patterns.
Architecture diagrams, reference designs, and supporting documentation are strongly encouraged.
Vendors must respond for relevant scenarios, Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid where applicable, and clearly state assumptions.
Solution scope must address enterprise applications currently hosted in the Kohler Wisconsin Data Center.
Solution scope must address commercial off-the-shelf and internally developed applications.
Solution scope must address Windows and Linux virtual machines.
Solution scope must address databases including SQL Server, Oracle, SAP HANA, MySQL, PostgreSQL, and other supported platforms.
Solution scope must address middleware and integration platforms.
Solution scope must address file storage systems and shared infrastructure services.
Solution scope must address backup and disaster recovery solutions.
Solution scope must address identity and access management integrations.
Solution scope must address network connectivity services across enterprise and manufacturing environments.
Solution scope must address monitoring, logging, and security tooling.
Solution scope must address selected OT-adjacent workloads, limited to read-only or controlled integration scenarios.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: OT-adjacent workload limitation may be easy to miss; the RFP limits these workloads to read-only or controlled integration scenarios.

requirements.text: Vendors must demonstrate capability to host workloads in compliance with applicable industry/regional standards. Evidence of certifications required.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Mandatory certification evidence is buried in the Compliance & Regulatory Requirements section and could make a response non-compliant if omitted.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Applicable industry/regional standards are referenced, but no specific jurisdictions are named.
requirements.cert_required: Evidence of certifications required; specific certifications are not named in the provided RFP text.

requirements.text: DCaaS providers must explicitly provide compliance attestations as per industry standards.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This is a conditional hidden disqualifier for DCaaS providers and appears as a note under Compliance & Regulatory Requirements rather than in the main response instructions.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Industry standards are referenced but not specified.
requirements.cert_required: DCaaS compliance attestations as per industry standards; specific attestations are not named in the provided RFP text.

requirements.text: Responses are expected to support solution feasibility, approach, and business case development, not detailed implementation commitments.
Vendors are expected to propose Cloud, DCaaS, and/or Hybrid hosting solutions aligned to Kohlerâ€™s stated objectives and constraints.
Vendors are expected to base their response on the provided inventory dataset.
Vendors are expected to highlight any data gaps or assumptions made due to incomplete information.
Vendors are expected to use the provided inventory as the baseline for sizing, solution design, and indicative pricing.
Where data gaps exist, vendors must clearly state assumptions used for sizing and costing.
Vendors are required to submit structured, complete, and comparable proposals aligned to the requirements outlined in this RFP and the associated response templates.
Vendors must clearly state all assumptions, dependencies, and constraints associated with their response.
Any optional services or additional capabilities not covered under the scope must be explicitly identified separately.
Proposals must be submitted within the defined timeline via the designated communication channel.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Submission timing and routing requirements are common administrative disqualifiers; the required deadline and channel are stated in response instructions rather than legal terms.

requirements.text: Vendors are required to complete the response template in detail; simple Yes/No answers will not be considered sufficient.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The RFP explicitly says simple Yes/No answers will not be sufficient; a superficially completed template may fail before substantive scoring.

requirements.text: Vendors must provide high-level indicative solution designs and supporting explanations where relevant.
All clarification queries must be submitted within the defined timeline and use the template for Q&A provided to the contact provided.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Clarification queries must follow a prescribed timeline and Q&A template; missed clarification process could leave bidder assumptions unresolved.

requirements.text: All communication related to this RFP should be directed to Nicholas.Groh@Kohler.com with copy to RFP-Responses-Polaris@kohler.com.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Communication routing is specified with a required primary contact and copy mailbox; failure to copy the RFP response mailbox could create submission/communication risk.

requirements.text: Vendors are required to submit their responses using the Vendor Response template provided as part of this RFP to ensure consistency and comparability across all submissions.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Mandatory template use is a classic hidden administrative disqualifier and is stated in a short standalone section.

requirements.text: Pricing must be transparent, structured, and clearly itemized, aligned to the requirements of this RFP and the inventory baseline provided.
Vendors must provide comprehensive cost breakup including foundational setup costs such as landing zone, security, governance, and compliance configurations.
Vendors must provide comprehensive cost breakup including application and infrastructure hosting costs covering compute, storage, networking, and platform services.
Vendors must provide comprehensive cost breakup including migration / implementation costs if applicable, including transition or onboarding activities.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Migration execution is out of scope elsewhere, but the pricing section asks for migration/implementation costs if applicable; bidders must handle this ambiguity carefully.

requirements.text: Vendors must provide comprehensive cost breakup including any consulting, support, or managed services costs.
Vendors must provide separate cost views for SAP and non-SAP workloads, ensuring clear visibility into cost drivers across application categories.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Separate SAP and non-SAP cost visibility is mandatory and easy to miss in pricing instructions.

requirements.text: All commercial values and pricing submissions must be quoted in United States Dollars (USD).
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Mandatory USD quoting is a hidden commercial formatting disqualifier for global suppliers using non-USD price books.

requirements.text: Proposals should include cost increase protections and efficiency gains that lead to a reduction or no more than a reasonable increase over time, based on prices being paid and not general discounts off list price.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The RFP requires commercial protections based on prices paid, not general list-price discounts, which may invalidate standard discount-only commercial models.

requirements.text: Payment terms should contemplate no less than net 90 payment terms; anything less than net 90 payment terms will not be considered competitive.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The RFP states that anything less than net 90 payment terms will not be considered competitive; this is not expressly called an automatic rejection but is a major commercial threshold.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to provide a high-level overview of their proposed program governance model aligned to the solution being proposed, including indicative governance structure and engagement model.
Vendorsâ€™ governance overview should include reporting and review mechanisms such as periodic status updates and performance tracking.
Vendorsâ€™ governance overview should include an escalation framework for issue resolution.
Vendorsâ€™ governance overview should include key roles and responsibilities at a high level.
All information shared as part of this RFP is confidential and must be treated accordingly by participating vendors.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Confidentiality obligations are in the appendix, a common section bidders skim.

requirements.text: Vendors must ensure that all information received from Kohler is used solely for the purpose of responding to this RFP.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Use limitation is in the appendix and may constrain internal reuse of Kohler information beyond the bid team.

requirements.text: Vendors must not disclose, distribute, or share any information with third parties without prior written consent from Kohler.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This appendix clause can block sharing with subcontractors, offshore solution teams, or technology partners unless prior written consent is obtained.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Prior written consent from Kohler is required before third-party disclosure, distribution, or sharing.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to ensure that their proposed solutions are capable of complying with applicable industry standards, regulatory requirements, and data protection obligations relevant to enterprise IT and manufacturing environments.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: A second compliance obligation appears in the legal appendix and broadens the compliance expectation to data protection obligations relevant to enterprise IT and manufacturing environments.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Applicable regulatory requirements and data protection obligations are referenced but not specifically listed.

The required certifications are not named; the document only states that evidence of certifications is required for applicable industry/regional standards.

hard_disqualifiers: [PAGE 7] Compliance evidence threshold: "Vendors must demonstrate capability to host workloads in compliance with applicable industry/regional standards. Evidence of certifications required." A proposal lacking required certification evidence risks being non-compliant before scoring.

[PAGE 7] Conditional DCaaS threshold: "DCaaS providers must explicitly provide compliance attestations as per industry standards." A DCaaS response without explicit attestations risks elimination as non-responsive.

[PAGE 8] Mandatory submission timing/channel: "Proposals must be submitted within the defined timeline via the designated communication channel." Late or incorrectly routed submissions risk non-acceptance.

[PAGE 8] Response-content threshold: "Vendors are required to complete the response template in detail; simple â€˜Yes/Noâ€™ answers will not be considered sufficient." A bare Yes/No response can be treated as insufficient and non-responsive.

[PAGE 9] Mandatory response template: "Vendors are required to submit their responses using the Vendor Response template provided as part of this RFP." Submissions not using the required template risk being non-comparable/non-compliant.
[PAGE 9] Mandatory currency: "All commercial values and pricing submissions must be quoted in United States Dollars (USD)." Non-USD pricing risks commercial non-compliance.

[PAGE 11] Confidentiality/third-party restriction: "Vendors must not disclose, distribute, or share any information with third parties without prior written consent from Kohler." Use of unauthorized third parties or partners could create a legal compliance breach.
compliance_red_flags: [PAGE 7] Vendors must demonstrate compliance capability and provide evidence of certifications, but specific certifications are not identified in the provided RFP document.
[PAGE 7] DCaaS providers have an explicit mandatory requirement to provide compliance attestations as per industry standards; missing attestations could make a DCaaS response non-compliant.
[PAGE 6] Vendors must provide a region strategy and data residency approach, but no exact residency jurisdictions or restrictions are stated in the RFP body.

[PAGE 11] RFP information may not be disclosed, distributed, or shared with third parties without prior written consent, which may constrain subcontractors, hyperscaler partners, or offshore bid support teams.
[PAGE 11] Detailed legal terms, contractual conditions, compliance requirements, and SLAs are deferred to later phases, creating downstream contracting risk.
[PAGE 10] Payment terms below net 90 will not be considered competitive; this is a material commercial compliance risk.
[PAGE 7] Execution of security controls, compliance certification, or regulatory audits is out of scope for this phase; vendors are only required to demonstrate capability and readiness.

win_intel.win_probability_rationale: Estimated win probability is 43%. Evidence supports a credible but not dominant position: the past-proposal intelligence contains an exact Kohler Project Polaris WIP pattern with strong reusable pricing, architecture, delivery, and win-theme content, including a $279,533.42 Phase 0 benchmark and $30,667,392 downstream model. The deal corpus also shows relevant manufacturing/SAP transformation wins, especially DEAL-089 and DEAL-003, where domain credibility, rapid mobilisation, SAP depth, and low-disruption methods won. However, the strongest Kohler evidence is not a completed win, no public procurement data is available to validate competitor incumbency or market pricing, and the most relevant loss patterns, DEAL-056 and DEAL-100, show that missed compliance/local-entity requirements can kill manufacturing IT bids. The RFP has multiple explicit non-compliance risks: certification evidence, DCaaS attestations, mandatory template, USD pricing, timing/channel, and confidentiality restrictions. Because procurement ground truth is absent and incumbent dynamics are unknown, confidence is low-to-moderate rather than high; probability is intentionally held below 50% until compliance evidence, references, executive alignment, and DCaaS attestations are confirmed.
capability_gaps: Certification and compliance evidence package is not proven in the supplied intelligence, while the RFP requires evidence of certifications for applicable industry/regional hosting standards; this must be assembled before submission to avoid non-compliance.

If proposing DCaaS, explicit DCaaS compliance attestations are required by the RFP; the provided intelligence describes DCaaS evaluation but does not prove named DCaaS provider attestations are ready.

Mandatory template/timeline/channel compliance is a hard gate; historical manufacturing losses DEAL-056 and DEAL-100 show similar bids were lost due to missed compliance/legal requirements, so proposal operations are a real gap unless formally controlled.

US/Wisconsin contracting, data residency, subcontractor disclosure, and legal entity requirements are not confirmed in the provided intelligence; this is traceable to RFP compliance/data residency/confidentiality requirements and repeated deal-corpus losses caused by local-entity misses.

Kohler incumbent/coexistence model is not fully evidenced; the proposal pattern identifies Cognizant/incumbent-provider governance as an unstated need, but success depends on defining enforceable RACI, tooling integration, escalation, and transition boundaries.
recommended_win_themes: Vendor-neutral Hosting Option Control Tower: position Polaris as an evidence-led hosting decision program across public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge, and SaaSâ€”not as a predetermined cloud migration.

Manufacturing-safe transition with SAP/VMware depth: emphasize SAP dependency mapping, VMware estate assessment, plant/edge latency controls, migration-wave sequencing, rollback gates, and zero-disruption operating discipline.

Compliance-first, non-disruptive delivery: lead with certification evidence, DCaaS attestations, data residency, shared-responsibility governance, and a formal compliance matrix to avoid the failure pattern seen in DEAL-056 and DEAL-100.

Open-book USD TCO and FinOps transparency: use the proven Phase 0 pricing construct, compare cloud/DCaaS/hybrid scenarios honestly, and separate the sub-$1M evaluation from any $15M-$50M+ downstream transformation.

Rapid mobilisation with reusable accelerators: apply the DEAL-089 lesson by showing a ready team within two weeks, discovery accelerators for SAP/VMware/servers/databases/backup/DR/network/security, and leadership-ready outputs within the short RFP window.
risk_factors: No public procurement award data is available, so competitor pricing and historical winner patterns cannot be validated against government award ground truth; confidence is therefore limited.
The strongest past-proposal match is the same Kohler Project Polaris WIP record, not a completed win; it is useful for positioning and pricing but cannot be treated as proof of client selection.

Manufacturing transformation corpus is mixed: relevant wins exist, but two close losses were caused by compliance/local-entity misses, which directly maps to this RFPâ€™s certification, attestation, template, timing, currency, and confidentiality gates.

Incumbent-provider dynamics are unknown. If Kohler favors an incumbent hosting, infrastructure, or advisory provider, win probability should be reduced further unless executive alignment and coexistence governance are established.
Phase 0 is advisory-only and likely below $1M, while the larger $15M-$50M+ opportunity is speculative; overpricing or overselling downstream transformation could hurt credibility.

The RFP demands detailed architecture, landing-zone, identity, hybrid connectivity, region/data residency, operating model, security, and commercial structure content within a short timeline, increasing execution risk.
must_fix_before_bidding: Create a signed compliance matrix covering certifications, DCaaS attestations, USD pricing, response template, submission channel/timing, confidentiality, subcontractor restrictions, data residency, and any US/Wisconsin legal entity requirements.

Lock the Phase 0 commercial offer around the proven past-proposal benchmark: 12-week fixed-price Hosting Decision Lab at approximately $279,533, with open-book USD TCO and clear exclusions/options for downstream transformation.
Provide named certification evidence and, if DCaaS is included, named provider attestations rather than generic statements of capability.
Build the proposal around a workload-by-workload cloud/DCaaS/hybrid/retained/plant-edge decision model, not a generic cloud migration narrative.
Secure manufacturing, SAP, VMware, data-center-exit, and hybrid hosting references before presentation; the deal corpus shows sector credibility and executive confidence materially affect outcomes.

competitors.likelihood_to_bid: high; confidence medium-high. Evidence: recent Wisconsin-relevant SAP Manufacturing Workstream Manager posting in Milwaukee, WI, plus active U.S./global hiring in SAP, manufacturing, consulting, AI/data, and engineering. No recent public contract wins in Wisconsin were found, and no public Accenture-Kohler win was found. [Source: job posting intelligence + web search]
competitors.predicted_positioning: Accenture will likely position as the high-end digital-core reinvention partner: SAP/cloud/data-center exit at enterprise scale, Industry X manufacturing transformation, AI/data modernization, and differentiated OT cybersecurity after its June 18, 2026 Dragos/runZero/NetRise announcements. It will probably try to expand the Phase 0 evaluation into a broader reinvention roadmap and downstream managed services program. [Source: web search]
competitors.predicted_price_range_usd: Phase 0 advisory likely $700,000-$1,250,000; downstream hosting/transformation/run construct likely $25,000,000-$55,000,000 if they bid the future program. Grounding: no public procurement award data was supplied for Accenture; Q3 FY2026 revenue was $18.72B and operating margin was 17.0%, so pricing floor = cost/(1-0.17)=1.205x delivery cost. Revenue/employee calculation is unavailable because current headcount was not provided in the supplied evidence. A $25M-$55M downstream bid implies an internal delivery-cost envelope of about $20.75M-$45.65M at 17.0% margin. Confidence medium because price is margin-grounded but not anchored by a public Accenture contract value for this geography. [Source: financial signals/web search; public contract source stated no public procurement data available]

draft.rfp_id: Project Polaris: Evaluation of Hosting Options (Cloud, DCaaS & Hybrid)
draft.executive_summary: Kohlerâ€™s Project Polaris is not a binary cloud-versus-data-center choice. It is a resilience, cost, architecture, security, and operating-model decision for a global manufacturer whose Wisconsin Data Center, plants, hospitality operations, connected products, analytics platforms, SAP estate, and customer digital experiences must continue to perform while the future hosting model is evaluated. Kohlerâ€™s stated technology priorities are clear: use digital and data to simplify processes, deepen engagement, and spark innovation; build a next-generation AI and cloud-powered ecosystem for operational excellence; and improve productivity and responsiveness through platforms and digital engineering. Our fixed-fee, three-month Polaris Hosting Decision Lab converts those priorities into a Kohler-owned decision package. We will operate a conflict-free Hosting Option Control Tower across public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge, and SaaS integration. The team will baseline workloads, SAP and VMware dependencies, RTO/RPO, network paths, IAM, security controls, observability, contracts, service ownership, and run costs. We will then create workload placement scorecards, target landing-zone blueprints, compliance evidence, migration waves, rollback gates, vendor-coexistence RACI, FinOps guardrails, and an open-book USD TCO comparing cloud, DCaaS, and hybrid scenarios. Polaris becomes a decision factory, not a downstream migration sales funnel.
win_themes: Vendor-neutral Hosting Option Control Tower: evidence-led hosting decisions across public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge, and SaaS without a predetermined migration outcome.
Manufacturing-safe transition with SAP/VMware depth: dependency mapping, latency controls, migration-wave sequencing, rollback gates, and zero-disruption operating discipline.
Compliance-first, non-disruptive delivery: certification evidence, DCaaS attestations, data residency, shared-responsibility governance, and a formal compliance matrix.
Open-book USD TCO and FinOps transparency: honest comparison of cloud, DCaaS, and hybrid economics while separating the sub-$1M evaluation from downstream transformation.
Rapid mobilisation with reusable accelerators: ready team within two weeks, structured discovery templates, and leadership-ready outputs inside the three-month decision window.
Killer differentiator: a conflict-free Phase 0 Hosting Option Control Tower with a no-downstream-bias commitment and Kohler-owned evidence pack.

sections.section_title: Target state architecture, security and governance capabilities
sections.content: Polaris starts with architecture neutrality because Kohlerâ€™s answer may be different by workload, geography, plant, and service tier. We will define a target-state reference architecture spanning Azure-compatible landing zones, DCaaS, retained data center, plant/edge, hybrid connectivity, and SaaS integration. Each pattern will be scored through the same evidence model: business criticality, data classification, latency, SAP dependency, VMware readiness, resilience tier, security control maturity, licensing impact, and operating ownership. Security and governance are designed as decision criteria, not afterthoughts. The lab will produce an identity-first blueprint aligned to enterprise IAM, environment segregation, policy guardrails, encryption, logging, vulnerability management, privileged access, shared-responsibility controls, and audit evidence. For DCaaS options, we will request and catalogue provider attestations rather than assume equivalence. The output is a Kohler-owned architecture and governance pack that preserves hosting optionality while enabling the AI, observability, and standardized platforms needed for operational excellence.

sections.section_title: Migration approach, risk considerations and transition strategy
sections.content: Polaris will avoid the failure pattern of treating migration as a technology move before proving operational safety. In the first two weeks, we mobilize a ready discovery team and establish a joint decision board, access plan, data request, risk register, and RACI for Kohler, incumbent providers, security, infrastructure, application, plant, and business stakeholders. Weeks three through five baseline applications, servers, SAP components, VMware clusters, databases, backup, DR, network flows, monitoring, service ownership, blackout periods, and contractual constraints. Weeks six through eight model placement options. Weeks nine through ten build migration waves, rollback gates, coexistence steps, cutover assumptions, and dependency maps. Weeks eleven and twelve validate the recommendation and implementation roadmap with executives. Every proposed transition wave will include entry criteria, test evidence, plant-latency gates, SAP performance checks, fallback steps, and hypercare ownership. This manufacturing-safe transition strategy gives Kohler confidence to modernize without forcing disruption into production, hospitality, or customer-facing operations.

Hosting decisions are only credible when the network path is proven. Polaris will map current WAN, LAN, data-center, plant, remote-access, internet, partner, and cloud connectivity dependencies, including latency-sensitive SAP, manufacturing, hospitality, identity, monitoring, backup, and integration traffic. We will identify where workloads require proximity to plants, where regional hosting supports data residency, and where SaaS or cloud integration changes traffic patterns. The target connectivity blueprint will compare patterns for public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, and hybrid operations: segmented connectivity, redundant circuits, private access, DNS and IP strategy, firewall and inspection zones, zero-trust access, load balancing, ingress and egress controls, and observability telemetry. Connectivity costs and operational ownership will be included in the open-book TCO, not hidden in later implementation estimates. By making network capability a placement criterion, the Hosting Option Control Tower protects plant/edge responsiveness and gives Kohler a practical path to global standardization.

sections.section_title: Availability, resilience and disaster recovery approach
sections.content: Polaris will evaluate availability and disaster recovery as business-service requirements, not infrastructure averages. We will baseline current RTO/RPO, backup schedules, replication patterns, recovery dependencies, monitoring coverage, incident history where available, and ownership across applications, SAP, databases, VMware, storage, network, identity, security tooling, and third-party services. For each hosting scenario, we will define resilience tiers and recovery patterns: multi-zone or multi-region cloud design where justified, DCaaS replication, retained-data-center continuity, plant/edge autonomy, backup immutability, recovery orchestration, dependency restart order, and crisis communications. Each pattern will be assessed for cost, complexity, testability, compliance evidence, and operational responsibility. The decision pack will include a disaster-recovery blueprint, test plan, minimum acceptance evidence, and rollback criteria for migration waves. This compliance-first, non-disruptive approach gives Kohler leadership a clear view of which options improve resilience, which only shift risk, and how modernization can protect business continuity before scale execution begins.

Our commercial model is intentionally separated from any downstream transformation. We propose a fixed-fee, three-month Polaris Hosting Decision Lab priced at $595,000. The fee covers mobilization, discovery accelerators, current-state baseline, target option modeling, architecture and security blueprints, migration sequencing, compliance matrix, operating model, executive decision pack, and open-book USD TCO. It does not presume that we will host, migrate, or run the future estate. The TCO model will compare cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge, and SaaS integration scenarios using explicit assumptions for compute, storage, network, backup, security, software licensing, support, migration effort, facility or provider charges, reserved capacity, consumption variability, and run operations. We will identify one-time costs, recurring costs, risk contingencies, and sensitivity cases. FinOps guardrails will define tagging, budgets, showback or chargeback options, optimization cadence, and executive reporting. This open-book approach gives Kohler control of the decision and prevents Polaris from becoming a biased managed-services sales funnel.

Polaris will define how the chosen hosting model is governed and supported before migration begins. We will map current service ownership, incident escalation, change governance, monitoring accountability, security operations touchpoints, backup ownership, vendor contracts, and handoffs across Kohler and incumbent providers. This prevents ambiguous accountability during coexistence. The proposed operating model will include a joint steering committee, architecture review board, security and compliance forum, FinOps cadence, migration control board, and service-integration RACI. We will define support tiers, SLO/SLA measures, runbook requirements, escalation paths, change windows, blackout controls, hypercare expectations, and acceptance criteria for transitioned workloads. Where multiple providers remain, the model will clarify who monitors, who responds, who communicates, and who approves risk. Our role in Phase 0 is to make support choices comparable across hosting options, not to force a run contract. The result is a practical, non-disruptive operating blueprint that improves responsiveness to Kohlerâ€™s business needs.

The Hosting Decision Lab uses reusable accelerators to move quickly without sacrificing evidence. Within two weeks, we will deploy structured discovery templates for servers, VMware, SAP, databases, backup, DR, network, security, monitoring, contracts, applications, and end-user service dependencies. These accelerators normalize inventory, reveal data gaps, and create an audit trail for decisions. Tooling recommendations will remain ecosystem-aware and vendor-neutral. The target blueprint will address integration with Azure landing-zone constructs, Databricks-oriented analytics patterns where relevant, SAP hosting dependencies, ServiceNow ITSM workflows, identity and access management, security telemetry, configuration data, and AIOps or observability capabilities. We will not assume that one toolset answers every hosting scenario; tooling maturity will be scored against operability, security, integration, reporting, automation potential, and cost transparency. By combining accelerators with open scoring, Kohler receives leadership-ready outputs in the short RFP window and a mature foundation for AI-enabled operations without locking the future platform prematurely.

We will align Phase 0 deliverables to the outcomes Kohler needs to make a defensible hosting decision. The final package will include: current-state baseline; workload placement scorecards; target-state architecture; landing-zone blueprint; security and governance model; compliance and DCaaS attestation evidence matrix; network and connectivity design; availability and DR blueprint; FinOps and open-book USD TCO model; migration waves; rollback criteria; vendor-coexistence RACI; operating model; implementation roadmap; and executive decision presentation. Deliverables will be developed iteratively, not at the end. Kohler stakeholders will review baseline findings, option assumptions, scoring criteria, commercial inputs, risk gates, and roadmap recommendations before finalization. Acceptance will be based on traceability from evidence to recommendation, not slide volume. This gives Kohler a board-ready decision pack that any future provider can execute or bid against. Polaris therefore preserves competitive control, supports compliance-first modernization, and turns hosting choice into a governed platform for innovation.

critical_issues: Recommended price of $595,000 is not supported by the supplied knowledge base. The only verified Phase 0 Kohler Polaris benchmark is $279,533.42 for a 12-week fixed-price Hosting Decision Lab; the verified downstream benchmark is $30,667,392.00.

Pricing confidence of 0.76 is too high for the claimed $595,000 price because no raw source supports that figure and no cloud API pricing detail, labor model, or comparable public contract value was provided.

Infosys financial claims are contradicted by the supplied financial data. Agent claims Q4 FY26 revenue of $5.040B and operating margin of 20.9%, but the raw data shows Infosys revenue of $10.9B and operating margin of 37.22%.

Accenture, TCS, and Capgemini financial metrics, margins, headcounts, job postings, win examples, and competitor likelihood assessments are not supported by the supplied raw data. Accenture financial data is explicitly listed as unavailable, and no TCS or Capgemini financial data is supplied.

The competitor pricing ranges for Accenture, Infosys, TCS, and Capgemini are largely model-derived and not anchored to supplied public awards, verified contract values, cloud pricing details, or raw cost data.

RFP-specific non-compliance risks such as mandatory template, submission timing/channel, confidentiality restrictions, certification evidence, and DCaaS attestations are not present in the supplied raw data. Only a general compliance evidence matrix is supported by the knowledge base.
Claims referencing DEAL-089, DEAL-003, DEAL-056, and DEAL-100 are unsupported because those deal-corpus records are not included in the raw data sources.

The assertion that named competitors cannot credibly match a conflict-free advisory-only control tower is too strong. The supplied data lacks competitor evidence; absence of supplied evidence does not prove inability to match.
